A configurator built to close the gap between the showroom floor and a screen.
Samsung Bespoke Design Studio — product configurator with carousel and colour selection interface

import HeroImage from '../../components/HeroImage.astro' import Meta from '../../components/Meta.astro' import ImagePair from '../../components/ImagePair.astro' import Carousel from '../../components/Carousel.astro' import ProseGroup from '../../components/ProseGroup.astro' import Figure from '../../components/Figure.astro' import ZigzagSection from '../../components/ZigzagSection.astro' import AccentBand from '../../components/AccentBand.astro' import heroSrc from '../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/scene-2-1.png' # Samsung Bespoke Design Studio Making a complex configurator feel simple through sequencing. Samsung's Bespoke line lets customers configure appliances down to the panel finish, but the online experience wasn't delivering the same sense of control as the showroom floor. I owned visual design for a tool that had to guide users through dozens of decisions without overwhelming them. ![Desktop view of the Bespoke Design Studio](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/desktop-06.png) ![Mobile view of the Bespoke Design Studio](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/mobile-06.png) ## The Problem A customer configuring a Bespoke fridge has to pick a model, then a size, then colours for four separate door panels. Show all of that at once and people bail — the early prototypes confirmed this. Show too little and they don't understand their options. The core challenge was progressive disclosure: finding the right order to present decisions so each step felt obvious, not overwhelming. In-store, a salesperson sequences those choices naturally. Online, the interface has to do that work. ![Desktop configuration view](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/desktop-03b.png) ![Mobile configuration view](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/mobile-04b.png) ## The Work I owned visual design — not strategy or UX research, but the surface layer where those decisions become real to a user. The key design decision was a stepped progress indicator that showed users the scope of remaining decisions upfront. That single addition cut abandonment in the configurator's mid-flow, because users could see they were three steps from done rather than wondering if the choices would ever end. ![Desktop detail view](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/desktop-04b.png) ![Mobile detail view](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/mobile-03b.png) ## Wireframes Multiple rounds of wireframing explored how to sequence the configuration steps. The core tension: each decision depended on the previous one (you can't pick panel colours until you've chosen a model), but users needed to understand the full scope before committing to step one. ![Wireframe slide 1](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/slide1.webp) ![Wireframe slide 2](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/slide2.webp) ![Wireframe slide 3](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/model.webp) ![Wireframe slide 4 depth](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/depth.webp) ![Wireframe slide 4](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/slide4.webp) ![Wireframe slide 5](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/slide5.webp) ![Wireframe slide 6](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/slide6.webp) ![User flow for Bespoke Kitchen](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/user-flow-bespoke-kitchen-2.jpg) ## User Flow The user flow mapped every decision point and branch. The stepped progress indicator — visible at every stage — was the key addition. It gave users a mental model of the whole journey before they started, which is what made the configurator feel manageable rather than endless. ![Kitchen configurator overview](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/ha-bespoke-kitchen-desktop-006.jpg) ## Design Execution I led UI and interaction design across mobile and desktop. Motion reinforced actions, educational touchpoints reduced hesitation, and the design system was built flexible enough to grow with the platform. Every visual decision served the sequencing — colour swatches appeared only when relevant, model comparisons showed only meaningful differences. ![Configurator color selection — choosing panel finishes by zone](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/image-2.png) ![Configurator with full color panel — all four door zones visible](../../assets/work/bespoke-design-studio/new-design.png) ## What I Learned The hardest part of this project was sequencing. Progressive disclosure sounds straightforward in theory, but in practice it's a series of trade-offs — every decision you defer to a later step is a decision the user might not reach. The stepped progress indicator worked because it gave users a mental model of the whole journey before they started. That's what made the configurator feel manageable rather than endless.
Design system and GNB for one of the world's most-visited consumer electronics sites.
Samsung.com redesign — global navigation and product pages across desktop and mobile

import HeroImage from '../../components/HeroImage.astro' import Meta from '../../components/Meta.astro' import ImagePair from '../../components/ImagePair.astro' import ProseGroup from '../../components/ProseGroup.astro' import Carousel from '../../components/Carousel.astro' import Figure from '../../components/Figure.astro' import ImageGrid from '../../components/ImageGrid.astro' import ZigzagSection from '../../components/ZigzagSection.astro' import AccentBand from '../../components/AccentBand.astro' import heroSrc from '../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/revamp-kv.jpg' # Samsung.com Redesign Working across a massive cross-functional team on a global platform, I contributed to three areas of Samsung.com: the design system, page layouts, and the global navigation bar. The GNB was the hardest problem — simplifying a navigation that had to work across 12+ markets without losing discoverability. ![UI mosaic overview](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/revamp-ui-mosaic.jpg) ## Design System I contributed atoms and components to Samsung's design system — building pieces that regional teams could compose into market-specific pages without breaking brand consistency. The system had to be strict enough to maintain visual cohesion across 12+ markets, but flexible enough to accommodate different content lengths, imagery, and promotional structures. ## The Problem Samsung.com's navigation had accumulated complexity from years of market-by-market additions. Different product catalogues meant different category hierarchies. Different languages meant different string lengths and overflow behaviours. Right-to-left scripts flipped the entire spatial logic. The existing GNB had grown organically and it showed — every market had its own workarounds. The challenge was designing a navigation architecture strict enough to read as one brand globally, but flexible enough that no market needed to break it. ![Product category page](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-2-pcd-desktop.jpg) ![Product detail zoom](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-2-pd-zoom-desktop.jpg) ## The Work As a Senior Designer at Razorfish, my role was fluid across the project. I designed GNB variations and prototyped them, built page layouts to Samsung's brand standards, and contributed design system atoms and components. For the GNB specifically, every iteration had to be stress-tested against the full range of script directions, catalogue depths, and nav item counts — not just a single locale. Most of the design work was about complexity reduction: the navigation had too many levels and options, and the challenge was simplifying without losing discoverability. ![Samsung.com homepage full page](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/revamp-hp.jpg) ## Page Layouts I designed page layouts across Samsung.com's core page types — homepage, product detail, product category, and product family — working within Samsung's rigid brand guidelines while ensuring each layout served its specific user intent. The homepage drove discovery, product pages drove purchase confidence, and category pages needed to handle wildly different catalogue sizes per market. ![Global navigation bar — mobile category browsing](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-3-gnb-desktop.jpg) ## Global Navigation The GNB was the hardest problem on the project. Samsung's navigation had too many levels and options, and the challenge was simplifying without losing discoverability. I designed variations and prototyped them, stress-testing each against the full range of script directions, catalogue depths, and nav item counts. The goal was a single navigation system strict enough that no market needed to break it to make it work. ![Global navigation closeup](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-3-gnb-closeup-desktop.jpg) ![Global navigation full desktop](../../assets/work/samsung-redesign/samsung-section-3-gnb-desktop-full.jpg) ## What I Learned The GNB problem was information architecture before it was visual design. Designing for the hardest case first — the most complex market's catalogue depth and script direction as the baseline — forced decisions about hierarchy, truncation, and overflow that a single-locale design would never surface. At global scale, the designer's job is to write constraints regional teams can work within, not to design every variant. A system that requires exceptions is a system that will drift.
A top-to-bottom mobile redesign that made a utility app feel intentional.
MyFrontier app redesign — mobile screens showing dashboard, bill pay, and support flow

import HeroImage from '../../components/HeroImage.astro' import Meta from '../../components/Meta.astro' import LottiePlayer from '../../components/LottiePlayer.astro' import ProseGroup from '../../components/ProseGroup.astro' import Figure from '../../components/Figure.astro' import Carousel from '../../components/Carousel.astro' import ImagePair from '../../components/ImagePair.astro' import ZigzagSection from '../../components/ZigzagSection.astro' import AccentBand from '../../components/AccentBand.astro' import heroSrc from '../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/frontier-app-kv.png' # MyFrontier App Redesign Motion that earns its place by solving specific user anxiety. The MyFrontier app covered bill pay, account management, technician scheduling, and support — but the experience didn't hold together. As ADD on a small team close to dev, I rebuilt the visual design, defined the layout system, and created a motion language from scratch to give users confidence at the moments they needed it most. ![Dashboard home](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/dashboard-1.png) ![Account overview](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/dashboard-2.png) ## Interface Design I designed every core screen — onboarding, dashboard, bill pay, and support. The layout system prioritised information hierarchy: the most important action on each screen got the most visual weight, and secondary actions were consistently grouped so users built muscle memory across flows. ![Splash screen](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/intro-1.png) ![Sign in](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/intro-2.png) ![Face ID prompt](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/intro-3.png) ![Sign in confirmed](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/intro-4.png) ## The Problem Nobody opens a telecom app to browse. They open it because they have a question about their bill, because a technician is coming and they need to confirm the window, or because their internet is down and they need answers fast. The MyFrontier app had accumulated inconsistencies across years: layouts that varied from flow to flow, controls that gave no feedback, and zero motion. Every fix had to respect those real-world stakes — people in a bad moment shouldn't have to think about the interface. ![Bill overview](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/bill-pay-1.png) ![Payment method](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/bill-pay-2.png) ![Payment date](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/bill-pay-3.png) ![Payment confirmed](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/bill-pay-4.png) ## The Work I owned layout design across every screen and defined visual hierarchy for core UI components — navigation, appointment calendar, and selector patterns throughout bill pay and account management. The structural decisions (information priority per screen, element sizing matched to confidence rather than just thumb reach, consistent control grouping) were what made the app feel like a single product. Alongside that, I built a motion system by mapping the specific uncertainty moments — "did my payment go through?", "is my appointment confirmed?" — and designing animations to resolve each one. ![Support home](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/support-1.png) ![Tech on the way](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/support-2.png) ![Installation in progress](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/support-3.png) ![All set](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/support-4.png) ![Modify appointment](../../assets/work/myfrontier-app/screens/support-5.png) ## Motion Design I built every Lottie file personally — After Effects compositions exported through the Lottie pipeline. Each animation was mapped to a specific uncertainty moment: the coin confirmed a payment was processing, the leaf signalled setup completion, and the intro sequence covered the initial data load so the app felt responsive from the first tap. I delivered motion specs alongside the files — easing values, timing, and documentation so dev could implement without guesswork.
## What I Learned In a utility app, the user's trust is already conditional — a telecom app can't afford animation that says "look at us." Each animation had to earn its place by solving a specific problem. That constraint produced a tighter, more purposeful motion system than most projects get. The motion specs and dev handoff documentation mattered as much as the animations themselves — without clear easing values and timing, the implementation would have drifted from the intent.
Design system foundations for one of the largest pharmacy retailers in the country.
CVS Shop website redesign — e-commerce homepage, navigation, and design system components

import HeroImage from '../../components/HeroImage.astro'
import Meta from '../../components/Meta.astro'
import ImagePair from '../../components/ImagePair.astro'
import ProseGroup from '../../components/ProseGroup.astro'
import Figure from '../../components/Figure.astro'
import ZigzagSection from '../../components/ZigzagSection.astro'
import AccentBand from '../../components/AccentBand.astro'
import heroSrc from '../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/cvs-header-2.jpg'

<HeroImage src={heroSrc} alt="CVS Shop Website Redesign — project showcase" />

<ProseGroup>

# CVS Shop Website Redesign

Building foundations that gave teams freedom rather than constraints. CVS.com is one of the largest pharmacy retailer e-commerce platforms in the US, and the platform had accumulated years of inconsistency across acquisitions. I owned the type system, the icon system, the component library, and the overall design direction — building composable primitives rather than page-specific components so teams could assemble freely.

</ProseGroup>

<Meta client="CVS" role="Associate Design Director" agency="Razorfish" year={2023} />

<ZigzagSection>
  <Fragment slot="media">
    ![Brand visual language — CVS design elements and UI patterns](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/group-10624.jpg)
  </Fragment>
  <ProseGroup>

  ## Typography & Icons

  I designed a type system with a rational scale and a grid-based icon and illustration system using CVS brand red. Each icon was constructed on a consistent grid so proportions held at any size — no one-off sizing decisions. The type scale and icon grid became the foundation everything else was built on.

  </ProseGroup>
</ZigzagSection>

<ProseGroup>

## The Problem

CVS sits at an unusual intersection — pharmacy and general retailer — which means the platform serves a wider range of intent than most e-commerce sites. Over time, the shop grew through acquisitions rather than deliberate system thinking, producing fractured type scales, inconsistent icons, and components built independently rather than from shared parts. Because CVS.com is a live platform with real customer volume, fixing it required discipline: I couldn't redesign from scratch, I had to build foundations that improved everything downstream without breaking what was already working.

</ProseGroup>

<ImagePair>
  <Fragment slot="left">![Icon and illustration system — geometric shapes built for the CVS brand](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10558.png)</Fragment>
  <Fragment slot="right">![Icon construction — grid-based build process for consistent sizing](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10629.png)</Fragment>
</ImagePair>

<ProseGroup>

## The Work

I chose composable primitives over page-specific components. That was the key decision — rather than designing a "homepage hero" or a "category card," I built atomic pieces (type scale, icon grid, spacing system) that any team could assemble into what they needed. Starting with the typographic framework meant every downstream component inherited consistency rather than having to engineer it in later. The icon system followed the same principle: every icon built on a grid, using CVS brand red, so proportions held at any size without case-by-case adjustment.

</ProseGroup>

<ZigzagSection reversed>
  <Fragment slot="media">
    ![CVS.com homepage — desktop and mobile responsive layout](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10669.png)
  </Fragment>
  <ProseGroup>

  ## Components & Templates

  The component library covered homepage modules, product pages, and category navigation — each piece built to work across desktop and mobile from the start, preventing the usual pattern of designing desktop first and adapting after the fact.

  </ProseGroup>
</ZigzagSection>

![Shop navigation — category browsing on desktop and mobile](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10672.png)

![Brand page template — Skin Care / Neutrogena on mobile and desktop](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/group-10604.png)

<ImagePair>
  <Fragment slot="left">![UI components — headlines, product cards, and product tabs](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10630.png)</Fragment>
  <Fragment slot="right">![UI components — pagination, branding elements, and category navigation](../../assets/work/cvs-redesign/frame-10608.png)</Fragment>
</ImagePair>

<ProseGroup>

## What I Learned

Foundation work is where the most leverage is. When the typographic scale is resolved, product teams stop making ad hoc sizing decisions. When the icon system is consistent, no one debates stroke weight. What composable primitives unlocked was speed downstream — consistent inputs produce consistent outputs, freeing design and engineering capacity for harder problems like checkout and pharmacy flows. The system gave teams freedom by removing the decisions that shouldn't vary.

</ProseGroup>
